original: Hljóðs bið ek allar helgar kindir, meiri ok minni mögu Heimdallar; viltu, at ek, Valföðr, vel fram telja forn spjöll fira, þau er fremst um man.

translations.bellows: Hearing I ask from the holy races, From Heimdall's sons, both high and low; Thou wilt, Valfather, that I should relate Old tales of the world, the earliest I know.
translations.thorpe: Give ear, all ye holy beings, Greater and lower sons of Heimdall; Thou, Valfather, wilt that I relate The old traditions of men, the earliest I remember.
translations.literal: Silence I ask of all holy kins, greater and lesser sons of Heimdall; you wish that I, Father of the Slain, well relate the ancient stories of men, those that I remember earliest.

original: Ek man jötna ár um borna, þá er forðum mik fædda höfðu; níu man ek heima, níu íviðjur, mjötvið mæran fyr mold neðan.

translations.bellows: I remember yet the giants of old, Who gave me bread in the days gone by; Nine worlds I knew, the nine wood-maidens, And the glorious dwelling-tree down in the earth.
translations.thorpe: I remember giants born of yore, Who in former times reared me; Nine worlds I remember, nine trees, The great central tree beneath the earth.
translations.literal: I remember giants early born, those who long ago fed me; nine worlds I remember, nine giantesses, the glorious world-tree before it was under the earth.

original: Ár var alda þar er Ymir byggði, vara sandr né sær né svalar unnir; jörð fannsk æva né upphiminn, gap var ginnunga, en gras hvergi.

translations.bellows: Old was the age when Ymir lived; Sea nor cool waves nor sand there were; Earth had not been, nor heaven above, But a yawning gap, and grass nowhere.
translations.thorpe: It was Time's earliest dawn when Ymir dwelt: there was no sand nor sea, nor cooling billows; earth was nowhere found, nor heaven above; a yawning chasm was there, but grass nowhere.
translations.literal: Early was the age when Ymir lived, there was no sand nor sea nor cold waves; earth was found nowhere nor the high-heaven, there was a ginnunga-gap, and grass nowhere.

original: Þór létu Burar synir bjöðum um yppa, þeir er miðgarð mæran sköpuðu; sól skein sunnan á salar steina, þá var grund groin grænum lauki.

translations.bellows: Then Bur's sons lifted the level land, Mithgarth the mighty there they made; The sun from the south warmed the stones of earth, And green was the ground with growing leeks.
translations.thorpe: Until Bur's sons raised the plains, they who the noble Midgard shaped. The sun shone from the south on the mansion's stones; then was the ground verdant with green leeks.
translations.literal: Until Bur's sons lifted the land-boards, those who Midgard the glorious shaped; Sun shone from the south on the hall's stones, then was the ground grown with green leeks.

original: Sól varp sunnan, sinni mána, hendi inni hægri um himinjódýr; sól þat ne vissi, hvar hon sali átti, stjörnur þat ne vissu, hvar þær staði áttu, máni þat ne vissi, hvat hann megins átti.

translations.bellows: The sun, the sister of the moon, from the south Her right hand cast over the heaven's edge; No knowledge she had where her home should be, The moon knew not what might was his, The stars knew not where their stations were.
translations.thorpe: The sun from the south, the moon's companion, her right hand cast round the heavenly horses. The sun knew not where she a dwelling had; the moon knew not what power he possessed; the stars knew not where they had a station.
translations.literal: Sun cast from the south, companion of the moon, her right hand around the heaven-horses; Sun knew not where she had halls, stars knew not where they had places, Moon knew not what might he had.

original: Þá gengu regin öll á rökstóla, ginnheilög goð, ok um þat gættusk; nótt ok niðjum nöfn um gáfu, morgun hétu ok miðjan dag, undorn ok aftan, árum at telja.

translations.bellows: Then sought the gods their assembly-seats, The holy ones, and council held; Names then gave they to noon and twilight, Morning and evening, night and mid-day, And the waning moon, for the year's full tale.
translations.thorpe: Then went all the powers to their judgment-seats, the all-holy gods, and thereon held council: to night and the waning moon gave names; morn they named, and mid-day, twilight and eve, for time-computation.
translations.literal: Then went all the powers to the doom-seats, very holy gods, and about that consulted; to night and the dark moon names they gave, morning they called and mid-day, afternoon and evening, to count the years.

original: Hittusk æsir á Iðavelli, þeir er hörg ok hof há timbruðu; afla lögðu, auð smíðuðu, tangir sköpuðu ok tól görðu.

translations.bellows: At Ithavoll met the mighty gods, Shrines and temples they timbered high; Forges they set, and they smithied ore, Tongs they wrought, and tools they fashioned.
translations.thorpe: The Aesir met on Ida's plain, they built altars and raised temples; edifices they reared, and gold fashioned; tongs they made, and tools fabricated.
translations.literal: Met the Aesir on the Field of Activity, they who altars and temples high timbered; forges they set, wealth they smithied, tongs they shaped and tools made.

original: Tefldu í túni, teitir váru, var þeim vettugis vant ór gulli, unz þrjár kömu þursa meyjar, ámáttkar mjök, ór Jötunheimum.

translations.bellows: In their dwellings at peace they played at tables, Of gold no lack did the gods then know; Till thither came up three giant-maids, Mighty of stature, from Jotunheim.
translations.thorpe: At tables they played in the court, and were merry; for them there was no lack of gold, until there came three giant maidens, very powerful, from Jotunheim.
translations.literal: They played at tables in the court, they were glad, there was to them no lack of gold; until three came, giant-maids, very powerful, from the home of giants.

original: Þá gengu regin öll á rökstóla, ginnheilög goð, ok um þat gættusk, hverr skyldi dverga dróttin skepja ór Brimis blóði ok ór Bláins leggjum.

translations.bellows: Then sought the gods their assembly-seats, The holy ones, and council held, To find who should shape the race of dwarfs Out of Brimir's blood and the legs of Blain.
translations.thorpe: Then went all the powers to their judgment-seats, the all-holy gods, and thereon held council, who should the dwarfs' mighty host create from Brimir's blood and from Blain's limbs.
translations.literal: Then went all the powers to the doom-seats, very holy gods, and about that consulted, who should of-dwarfs the host shape from Brimir's blood and from Blain's legs.

original: Þar var Mótsognir mæztr um orðinn dverga allra, en Durinn annarr; þeir manlíkun mörg gærðu dvergar í jörðu, sem Durinn sagði.

translations.bellows: Motsognir then was the worthiest deemed Of all the dwarfs, and Durin next; Many a likeness of men they made, The dwarfs in the earth, as Durin bade.
translations.thorpe: There was Motsognir the highest become of all the dwarfs, and Durin the next; they many images of men made, dwarfs in the earth, as Durin said.
translations.literal: There was Motsognir greatest become of-dwarfs all, and Durin second; they man-likenesses many made, dwarfs in earth, as Durin said.

original: Nýi ok Niði, Norðri ok Suðri, Austri ok Vestri, Alþjófr, Dvalinn, Bívurr, Bávurr, Bömburr, Nóri, Ánn ok Ánarr, Óinn, Mjöðvitnir.

translations.bellows: Nyi and Nithi, Northri and Suthri, Austri and Vestri, Althjof, Dvalin, Nar and Nain, Niping, Dain, Bifur, Bafur, Bombur, Nori, An and Onar, Ai, Mjothvitnir.
translations.thorpe: Nyi and Nidi, Nordri and Sudri, Austri and Vestri, Althjof, Dvalin, Na and Nain, Niping, Dain, Bifur, Bafur, Bombur, Nori, An and Anar, Ai, Mjodvitnir.
translations.literal: Nyi and Nidi, Northri and Suthri, Austri and Vestri, Althjof, Dvalin, Bivur, Bavur, Bombur, Nori, Ann and Annar, Oinn, Mjothvitnir.

original: Vigr ok Gandálfr, Vindálfr, Þráinn, Þekkr ok Þorinn, Þrór, Vitr ok Litr, Nár ok Nýráðr, nú hefi ek dverga, Reginn ok Ráðsviðr, rétt um talda.

translations.bellows: Vig and Gandalf, Vindalf, Thrain, Thekk and Thorin, Thror, Vit and Lit, Nár and Nyrath, now have I told—Regin and Rathsvith—the list aright.
translations.thorpe: Vig and Gandalph, Vindalph, Thrain, Thekk and Thorin, Thror, Vit and Lit, Nar and Nyrad, now have I these dwarfs—Regin and Radsvid—rightly enumerated.
translations.literal: Vig and Gandalf, Vindalf, Thrain, Thekk and Thorin, Thror, Vit and Lit, Nar and Nyrad, now have I dwarfs, Regin and Radsvid, rightly counted.

original: Fíli, Kíli, Fundinn, Njáli, Hepti, Víli, Hanarr, Svíurr, Billingr, Brúni, Bídr ok Balingr, Frár, Hornbori, Frægr ok Lóni, Aurvangr, Jari, Eikinskjaldi.

translations.bellows: Fili, Kili, Fundin, Nali, Hepti, Vili, Hanar, Svior, Frar, Hornbori, Fræg and Loni, Aurvang, Jari, Eikinskjaldi.
translations.thorpe: Fili, Kili, Fundin, Nali, Hepti, Vili, Hanar, Svior, Billing, Bruni, Bid, Balin, Frar, Hornbori, Fræg and Loni, Aurvang, Jari, Eikinskjaldi.
translations.literal: Fili, Kili, Fundin, Njali, Hepti, Vili, Hanar, Sviur, Billing, Bruni, Bid and Baling, Frar, Hornbori, Fræg and Loni, Aurvang, Jari, Eikinskjaldi.

original: Mál er dverga í drótti Dvalins foldar til fira fiskatal töldu, þeir er sóttu frá salar steini Aurvanga sjöt til Jöruvalla.

translations.bellows: The dwarfs in Dvalin's band it is time To tell to men, even down to Lofar; Who sought from the rocks the dwelling-place Of Aurvang's self to Joruvellir.
translations.thorpe: It is time that the dwarfs in Dvalin's band, to the sons of men, should Lofar reckon: they who sought from the stony mansion the seat of Aurvanga, to Joruvellir.
translations.literal: Time is of-dwarfs in host of-Dvalin for-men fish-list to-count, they who sought from hall's stone Aurvanga's seat to Joruvellir.

original: Þar var Draupnir ok Dolgþrasir, Hár, Haugspori, Hlévangr, Glói, Skirvir, Virvir, Skáfiðr, Ái, Álfr ok Yngvi, Eikinskjaldi.

translations.bellows: There were Draupnir and Dolgthrasir, Hor, Haugspori, Hlevang, Gloi, Dori, Ori, Duf, Andvari, Skirfir, Virfir, Skafith, Ai.
translations.thorpe: There was Draupnir and Dolgthrasir, Har, Haugspori, Hlevang, Gloi, Dori, Ori, Duf, Andvari, Skirfir, Virfir, Skafid, Ai.
translations.literal: There was Draupnir and Dolgthrasir, Har, Haugspori, Hlevang, Gloi, Skirvir, Virvir, Skafid, Ai, Alfr and Yngvi, Eikinskjaldi.

original: Fjalarr ok Frosti, Finnr ok Ginnarr, Heri, Höggstari, Hljóðólfr, Móinn; þat mun æ uppi, meðan öld lifir, langniðja tal Lofars hafat.

translations.bellows: Fjalar and Frosti, Finn and Ginnar, Heri, Hogstari, Hlövang, Moin; While mankind lives shall Lofar's list Of ancestors' names be ever known.
translations.thorpe: Fjalar and Frosti, Finn and Ginnar, Heri, Hogstari, Hlövang, Moin; that shall while mankind lives, the list of Lofar's ancestors be ever known.
translations.literal: Fjalar and Frosti, Finn and Ginnar, Heri, Hoggstari, Hljodolf, Moin; that will ever be up, while men live, ancestor-list of-Lofar held.

original: Unz þrjár kömu ór því liði, öflgir ok ástkir, æsir at húsi; fundu á landi, lítt megandi, Ask ok Emblu, örlögslausa.

translations.bellows: Then from the throng did three come forth, From the home of the gods, the mighty and kind; Ask and Embla unfashioned they found, Fate they had not, and force they lacked.
translations.thorpe: Until three Aesir from that host came, powerful and benevolent, to the sea-side, they found on the strand, helpless, Ask and Embla, without destiny.
translations.literal: Until three came from that host, powerful and loving, Aesir to the house; they found on land, little-mighty, Ask and Embla, fate-less.

original: Önd þeir né áttu, óð þeir né höfðu, lá né læti né litu góða; önd gaf Óðinn, óð gaf Hænir, lá gaf Loðurr ok litu góða.

translations.bellows: Soul they had not, sense they had not, Heat nor motion, nor goodly hue; Soul gave Odin, sense gave Hoenir, Heat gave Lothur and goodly hue.
translations.thorpe: Spirit they possessed not, sense they had not, blood nor motive powers, nor goodly color. Odin gave spirit, Hoenir gave sense, Lodur gave blood and goodly color.
translations.literal: Breath they had not, spirit they had not, blood nor voice nor goodly color; breath gave Odin, spirit gave Hoenir, blood gave Lodur and goodly color.

original: Ask veit ek standa, heitir Yggdrasill, hár baðmr, ausinn hvíta auri; þaðan koma döggvar þær er í dala falla, stendr æ yfir grænn Urðar brunni.

translations.bellows: An ash I know, Yggdrasil its name, With water white is the great tree wet; Thence come the dews that fall in the dales, Green by Urth's well does it ever grow.
translations.thorpe: I know an ash standing, named Yggdrasil, a lofty tree, laved with limpid water; thence come the dews into the dales that fall; ever stands it green over Urd's fountain.
translations.literal: An ash I know standing, called Yggdrasill, a high tree, sprinkled with white mud; thence come dews that in the dales fall; it stands ever green over Urd's well.

original: Þaðan koma meyjar margs vitandi þrjár, ór þeim sal er und þolli stendr; Urð hétu eina, aðra Verðandi, — skáru á skíði, — Skuld ina þriðju; þær lög lögðu, þær líf kuru alda börnum, örlög seggja.

translations.bellows: Thence come the maidens mighty in wisdom, Three from the dwelling down 'neath the tree; Urth is one named, Verthandi the next, On the slate they scored, and Skuld the third; Laws they made there, and life allotted To the sons of men, and set their fates.
translations.thorpe: Thence come maidens, much knowing, three from the hall, which under that tree stands; Urd they named one, Verthandi another, they on a tablet cut, Skuld the third; they laws established, they life allotted to the sons of men, the destinies of men.
translations.literal: Thence come maidens of much wisdom, three from the hall that under the tree stands; Urd they called one, Verthandi the second, they carved on wood-slips, Skuld the third; they laws laid, they lives chose for the children of men, the fates of men.

original: Fólkvíg man hon fyrst í heimi, er Gullveigu geirum studdu ok í höll Hárs hana brendu; þrisvar brendu, þrisvar borna, oft, ósjaldan, þó hon enn lifir.

translations.bellows: The war I remember, the first in the world, When the gods with spears had Gullveig pierced, And in the hall of Hor had burned her; Three times burned, and three times born, Oft and again, yet ever she lives.
translations.thorpe: She that war remembers, the first in the world, when they Gullveig with spears upheld, and in the High One's hall burned her; thrice burned, thrice born, oft, not seldom, yet she still lives.
translations.literal: Folk-war remembers she first in world, when Gullveig with spears they-propped and in hall of-Har her burned; thrice burned, thrice born, oft, not-seldom, though she yet lives.

original: Heidi hana hétu, hvar er til húsa kom, völu vel spáa, vitti hon ganda; seið hon, hvar er hon kunni, seið hon hug villan, æ var hon angan illrar brúðar.

translations.bellows: Heith they named her who sought their home, The wide-seeing witch, in magic wise; Minds she bewitched that were moved by her magic, To evil women a joy she was.
translations.thorpe: Heidi they called her, wheresoe'er she came, the well-prophesying Vala: she knotted magic spells; she lewdness practiced; she was ever the joy of evil people.
translations.literal: Heidi her they-called, where she to houses came, Vala well-prophesying, she-bewitched with-wands; she practiced seiðr, where she could, she practiced seiðr on-minds deluded, ever was she delight of-evil bride.

original: Þá gengu regin öll á rökstóla, ginnheilög goð, ok um þat gættusk, hvárt skyldu æsir afráð gjalda eða skyldu goðin öll gildi eiga.

translations.bellows: Then sought the gods their assembly-seats, The holy ones, and council held, Whether the gods should tribute give, Or to all alike should worship belong.
translations.thorpe: Then went all the powers to their judgment-seats, the all-holy gods, and thereon held council, whether the Aesir should a fine pay, or all the gods should tributes receive.
translations.literal: Then went all the powers to the doom-seats, very holy gods, and about that consulted, whether should Aesir tribute pay or should gods all worship have.

original: Fleygði Óðinn ok í fólk um skaut, þat var enn fólkvíg fyrst í heimi; brotinn var borðvegur borgar ása, knáttu vanir vígspá völlu sporna.

translations.bellows: Odin let fly, and a spear he flung In the thick of the host, the first of the war; Broken was then the wall of the gods, And the Vanir held the field of battle.
translations.thorpe: Odin cast his spear, and mid the people slung it; that was first war in the world. Broken was the outer wall of the Aesir's burg. The Vanir, foreseeing conflict, over the plains advance.
translations.literal: Flung Odin and into folk shot, that was yet folk-war first in world; broken was board-wall of-burg of-Aesir, could Vanir with-war-spell the-field tread.

original: Þá gengu regin öll á rökstóla, ginnheilög goð, ok um þat gættusk, hverr hefði loft alt lævi blandit eða ætt jötuns Óðs mey gefna.

translations.bellows: Then sought the gods their assembly-seats, The holy ones, and council held, To find who with venom the air had filled, Or had given Oth's bride to the giants' brood.
translations.thorpe: Then went all the powers to their judgment-seats, the all-holy gods, and thereon held council, who all the air with guile had blended, or to the giant race Odr's maid had given?
translations.literal: Then went all the powers to the doom-seats, very holy gods, and about that consulted, who had air all with-venom mixed or to-kin of-giant Od's maid given.

original: Ein var þar Þórr ofrunginn móði, hann sjaldan sitr, er hann slíkt of fregn; gengusk eiðar, orð ok særæri, mál öll meginlig, er á meðal fóru.

translations.bellows: Thor alone was swelled with rage, Rare sits he still when such things he hears; Bonds were broken, the binding oaths, And the pledges of might that were made between them.
translations.thorpe: Thor alone there was with resentment filled, he seldom sits when of the like he hears; broken were the oaths, the words, and promises, all the mighty ties between them formed.
translations.literal: One was there Thor swelled-with mood, he seldom sits when he such learns; broken-were oaths, words and solemn-vows, speeches all mighty, which between went.

original: Veit hon Heimdallar hljóð um folgit undir heiðvönum helgum baðmi; á sér hon ausask aurgan forsi af veði Valföðrs. Vituð ér enn — eða hvat?

translations.bellows: I know where Heimdall's horn is hid, Under the heaven-bright, holy tree; On it sits a stream in a foaming flood From Valfather's pledge. Would you know yet more?
translations.thorpe: She knows that Heimdall's horn is hidden under the heaven-bright holy tree. A river she sees flow with foamy fall, from Valfather's pledge. Understand ye yet, or what?
translations.literal: Knows she Heimdall's hearing hidden under heaven-bright holy tree; on it sees she poured muddy waterfall from pledge of-Valfather. Know you yet — or what?

original: Ein sat hon úti, þá er inn aldni kom, yggjungr ása, ok í augu leit: Hvers fregnið mik? Hví freistið mín? Alt veit ek, Óðinn, hvar þú auga falt: í inum mæra Mímis brunni.

translations.bellows: Alone I sat when the Old One sought me, The terror of gods, and gazed in mine eyes: 'What do you ask of me? Why do you tempt me?' Odin, I know where your eye is hidden, Deep in the wide-famed well of Mimir.
translations.thorpe: Alone she sat without, when the ancient Aesir's lord came, and in her eye looked: 'What ask ye me? why tempt ye me?' I know all, Odin, where thou thine eye didst sink in the pure well of Mimir.
translations.literal: Alone sat she out, when the old-one came, Ygg of-Aesir, and in eyes looked: What ask-you me? Why tempt-you me? All know I, Odin, where you eye hid: in the glorious Mimir's well.

original: Drekkr mjöð Mímir morgun hverjan af veði Valföðrs. Vituð ér enn — eða hvat?

translations.bellows: Mead from the pledge of Odin each morn Does Mimir drink. Would you know yet more?
translations.thorpe: Mimir drinks mead each morn from Valfather's pledge. Understand ye yet, or what?
translations.literal: Drinks mead Mimir morning each from pledge of-Valfather. Know you yet — or what?

original: Valði henni Herföðr hringa ok men, fekk spjöll spaklig ok spá ganda, sá hon vítt ok um vítt of veröld hverja.

translations.bellows: Necklaces had she and rings from Herfather, Wise was her spell and her magic seeds; Wide and far through the worlds she looked.
translations.thorpe: Herfather gave her rings and necklaces, useful discourse, and a divining spirit: wide and far she saw o'er every world.
translations.literal: Chose for-her Army-father rings and necklaces, got stories wise and prophecy of-wands, saw she wide and over wide over world each.

original: Ek sá Baldri, blóðgum tívur, Óðins barni, örlög fólgin; stóð um vaxinn völlum hærri, mjór ok mjög fagr, mistilteinn.

translations.bellows: I saw for Baldr, the bleeding god, The son of Odin, his destiny set; Famous and fair in the lofty fields, Full grown in strength the mistletoe stood.
translations.thorpe: I saw for Baldr, the blood-stained god, Odin's son, with fate concealed; there stood grown up, higher than the plains, slender and very fair, the mistletoe.
translations.literal: I saw for-Baldr, bloodied god, Odin's child, fate hidden; stood up-grown on-plains higher, slender and very fair, mistletoe.

original: Varð ór þeim meiði, er mær sýndisk, harmflaug hættlig, Höðr nam skjóta; Baldurs bróðir var of borinn snemma, sá nam Óðins sonr einnættr vega.

translations.bellows: From the branch which seemed so slender and fair Came a harmful shaft that Hoth should hurl; But the brother of Baldr was born ere long, And one night old, Odin's son slew him.
translations.thorpe: From that stem was made, as it appeared, a deadly-harmful dart: Hodr began to shoot. Baldr's brother was born betimes; he, Odin's son, at one night old, slew him.
translations.literal: Became from that branch, which slender seemed, a-harm-flight dangerous, Hodr took-to shoot; Baldr's brother was born early, he took Odin's son one-night-old to-slay.

original: Þó hann æva hendr né höfuð kembði, áðr á bál um bar Baldurs dólga; en Frigg um grét í Fensölum vá Valhallar. Vituð ér enn — eða hvat?

translations.bellows: His hands he washed not, his hair he combed not, Till he bore to the bale-fire Baldr's foe; But in Fensalir Frigg wept sore For Valhall's need. Would you know yet more?
translations.thorpe: He his hands washed not, nor his head combed, until he to the pile had borne Baldr's adversary. But Frigg wept in Fensalir for Valhall's calamity. Understand ye yet, or what?
translations.literal: Though he never hands nor head combed, before on bale-fire bore Baldr's-slayer; and Frigg wept in Fen-halls for-woe of-Valhalla. Know you yet — or what?

original: Haft sá hon liggja undir Hveralundi, lægjarnlíki Loka áþekkjan; þar sitr Sigyn, þeygi of sínum ver vel glýjuð. Vituð ér enn — eða hvat?

translations.bellows: In chains she saw one lying low Under Hveralund, like Loki in form; There sits Sigyn, for her husband's sake Not glad is she. Would you know yet more?
translations.thorpe: A captive she saw lying under Hveralund, in form like Loki, craftily-wicked. There sits Sigyn, for her lord's sake, not much rejoiced. Understand ye yet, or what?
translations.literal: Captive saw she lying under Kettle-grove, deceit-eager-form of-Loki like; there sits Sigyn, not over her husband well rejoiced. Know you yet — or what?

original: Á fellr austan um eitrdala söxum ok sverðum, Slíðr heitir sú.

translations.bellows: A river flows from the east through venom-vales With daggers and swords; Slith is its name.
translations.thorpe: A river flows from the east through venom-vales, with swords and daggers; Slid is its name.
translations.literal: River falls from-east through poison-vales with-knives and swords, Slid is-called that-one.

original: Stóð fyr norðan á Niðavöllum salr ór gulli Sindra ættar;

translations.bellows: Northward a hall in Nithavellir Of gold there rose for Sindri's race;
translations.thorpe: There stood in the north, on Nidavellir, a hall of gold, for Sindri's race;
translations.literal: Stood before north on Dark-fields hall of gold of-Sindri's kin;

original: en annarr stóð á Ókólni, bjórsalr jötuns, en sá Brimir heitir.

translations.bellows: And in Okolnir another stood, Where the giant Brimir his beer-hall had.
translations.thorpe: and another stood in Okolnir, the giant's beer-hall, who Brimir is named.
translations.literal: and another stood on Not-cold, beer-hall of-giant, and that-one Brimir is-called.

original: Sal sá hon standa sólu fjarri Náströndu á, norðr horfa dyrr; féllu eitrdropar inn um ljóra, sá er undinn salr orma hryggjum.

translations.bellows: A hall she saw standing far from the sun On Nastrond, and northwards its doors are turned; Venom-drops fall through the holes in the roof, For the hall is woven of serpents' backs.
translations.thorpe: A hall she saw standing far from the sun, on Nastrond; its doors look to the north. Venom-drops fall in through its apertures: that hall is woven of serpents' backs.
translations.literal: Hall saw she standing sun far-from, on Corpse-strand, north look doors; fell venom-drops in through roof-holes, that is woven hall of-serpents' backs.

original: Sá hon þar vaða þunga strauma menn meinsvara ok morðvarga ok þann er annars glepr eyrarúnu; þar saug Níðhöggr nái framgengna, sleit vargr vera. Vituð ér enn — eða hvat?

translations.bellows: There saw she wade through rivers wild Men who perjured themselves, and murderers too, And workers of ill with the wives of men; There Nithhogg sucked the blood of the slain, And the wolf tore men. Would you know yet more?
translations.thorpe: She saw there wading through turbid streams, perjured men and murderers, and him who another's wife seduces; there Nidhogg sucked the corpses of the departed; the wolf tore men. Understand ye yet, or what?
translations.literal: Saw she there wade heavy streams men oath-breakers and murder-wolves and that-one who another's whispers-ear-secrets [seduces]; there sucked Nidhogg corpses departed, tore wolf men. Know you yet — or what?

original: Austr sat in aldna í Járnviði ok fæddi þar Fenris kindir; verðr af þeim öllum einna nokkurr tungls tjúgari í trolls hami.

translations.bellows: In the east sat the old one, in Ironwood, And fed the offspring of Fenrir there; And of them all a monster shall come To rob the sun in a giant's shape.
translations.thorpe: Eastward sat the aged one in Jarnvid, and there reared Fenrir's progeny; of all these one shall become particularly the moon's devourer, in a troll's semblance.
translations.literal: Eastward sat the old-one in Iron-wood and fed there Fenrir's kin; becomes from them all one certain moon's snatcher in troll's skin.

original: Fyllisk fjörvi feigra manna, rýðr ragna sjöt rauðum blóði; svört verða sólskin of sumur eftir, veðr öll válynd. Vituð ér enn — eða hvat?

translations.bellows: He gorges the life of dying men, And the seats of the gods he stains with blood; Done is the sunshine through summers after, And all the winds are wild. Would you know yet more?
translations.thorpe: He is filled with the life's blood of men doomed to die; the gods' seats he reddens with ruddy gore; the sun-light is darkened in the summers following; all weather is changeful. Understand ye yet, or what?
translations.literal: Fills-himself with-life of-fey men, reddens gods' seats with-red blood; black become sun-shines over summers after, weather all treacherous. Know you yet — or what?

original: Sat þar á haugi ok sló hörpu gýgjar hirðir, glaðr Eggþér; gól of honum í galgviði fagrrauðr hani, sá er Fjalarr heitir.

translations.bellows: On a hill there sat, and smote on his harp, Eggther the joyous, the giants' warder; Above him the cock in the bird-wood crowed, Fair and red did Fjalar stand.
translations.thorpe: There sat on a mound, and struck his harp, the giantess's warder, the joyous Eggther; over him crowed in the bird-wood, a light-red cock, which Fjalar is named.
translations.literal: Sat there on mound and struck harp giantess's herder, glad Eggther; sang over him in gallow-wood fair-red cock, that-one who Fjalar is-called.

original: Gól of ásum Gullinkambi, sá vekr hölda at Herjaföðrs; en annarr gelr fyr jörð neðan sótrauðr hani at sölum Heljar.

translations.bellows: Then Gullinkambi sang to the gods, Who wakens the heroes in Herfather's hall; And another crows beneath the earth, A soot-red cock in the halls of Hel.
translations.thorpe: Gullinkambi crowed over the Aesir; he wakens the heroes in Herfather's hall; and another crows beneath the earth, a soot-red cock, in the halls of Hel.
translations.literal: Sang over Aesir Gold-comb, that-one wakes heroes at Army-father's; and another crows before earth beneath soot-red cock at halls of-Hel.

original: Geyr nú Garmr mjög fyr Gnipahelli, festr mun slitna, en freki renna; fjöld veit hon fræði, fram sé ek lengra um ragnarök römm sigtíva.

translations.bellows: Loud bays Garm before Gnipahellir; The bursting fetters shall free the wolf; Much do I know, and more can see Of the fate of the gods, the mighty in fight.
translations.thorpe: Garm bays terribly before the Gnipa-cave: the fetters will be burst, and the wolf run loose. Much she knows, more can see, of the Ragnarok, of the mighty gods of victory.
translations.literal: Bays now Garm much before Cliff-cave, bond will break, and wolf run; much knows she wisdom, forward see I further of Ragnarok mighty of-victory-gods.

original: Bræðr munu berjask ok at bönum verðask, munu systrungar sifjum spilla; hart er í heimi, hórdómr mikill, skeggöld, skálmöld, skildir ro klofnir, vindöld, vargöld, áðr veröld steypisk; mun engi maðr öðrum þyrma.

translations.bellows: Brothers shall fight and fell each other, And sisters' sons shall kinship stain; Hard is it on earth, with mighty whoredom; Axe-time, sword-time, shields are sundered, Wind-time, wolf-time, ere the world falls; Nor ever shall men each other spare.
translations.thorpe: Brothers shall fight, and slay each other; cousins shall kinship violate. The world is hard: adultery abounds; axe-age, sword-age, shields are cloven, wind-age, wolf-age, ere the world sinks. No man will spare another.
translations.literal: Brothers will fight and to slayers become, will sister's-sons kinship spoil; hard is in world, adultery great, axe-age, sword-age, shields are cloven, wind-age, wolf-age, before world falls; will no man another spare.

original: Leika Míms synir, en mjötuðr kyndisk at inu galla Gjallarhorni; hátt blæss Heimdallr, horn er á lofti, mælar Óðinn við Míms höfuð.

translations.bellows: Mimir's sons dance, and the measure is fated Under the sounding Gjallarhorn; Loud blows Heimdall, the horn is aloft, In fear doth Odin with Mimir's head speak.
translations.thorpe: Mimir's sons dance, but the world-tree takes fire, at the loud-sounding Gjallarhorn. Heimdall blows loud, his horn is in the air; Odin speaks with Mimir's head.
translations.literal: Play Mimir's sons, and fate is-kindled at the screaming Gjallarhorn; high blows Heimdall, horn is in air, speaks Odin with Mimir's head.

original: Skelfr Yggdrasils askr standandi, ymr it aldna tré, en jötunn losnar; hræðask allir á helvegum, áðr Surtar þann sefi of svelgr.

translations.bellows: Yggdrasil shakes, and shiver the woods, The ancient tree groans, and the giant is loose; To the head of Odin the heroes fly, Ere the flaming sword of Surt shall consume them.
translations.thorpe: Yggdrasil's ash shudders as it stands, the ancient tree groans, and the giant is loosed. All are terrified on the roads to Hel, before Surt's kinsman has consumed it.
translations.literal: Shakes Yggdrasil's ash standing, groans the old tree, and giant is-loose; fear-themselves all on Hel-ways, before Surt's that-one [fire] it swallows.

original: Hvat er með ásum? Hvat er með álfum? Gnýr allr Jötunheimr, æsir ro á þingi, stynja dvergar fyr steindurum, veggbergs vísir. Vituð ér enn — eða hvat?

translations.bellows: How fare the gods? how fare the elves? All Jotunheim groans, the gods are at council; The dwarfs before their stone doors groan, The masters of rocks. Would you know yet more?
translations.thorpe: How is it with the Aesir? How with the Alfar? All Jotunheim resounds; the Aesir are in council; the dwarfs groan before their stone doors, the sages of the rocky walls. Understand ye yet, or what?
translations.literal: What is with Aesir? What is with Elves? Roars all Giant-home, Aesir are at assembly, groan dwarfs before stone-doors, rock-wall's masters. Know you yet — or what?

original: Geyr nú Garmr mjög fyr Gnipahelli, festr mun slitna, en freki renna; fjöld veit hon fræði, fram sé ek lengra um ragnarök römm sigtíva.

translations.bellows: Loud bays Garm before Gnipahellir; The bursting fetters shall free the wolf; Much do I know, and more can see Of the fate of the gods, the mighty in fight.
translations.thorpe: Garm bays terribly before the Gnipa-cave: the fetters will be burst, and the wolf run loose. Much she knows, more can see, of the Ragnarok, of the mighty gods of victory.
translations.literal: Bays now Garm much before Cliff-cave, bond will break, and wolf run; much knows she wisdom, forward see I further of Ragnarok mighty of-victory-gods.

original: Hrymr ekr austan, hefisk lind fyrir, snýsk Jörmungandr í jötunmóði; ormr knýr unnir, en ari hlakkar, slítr nái neffölr, Naglfar losnar.

translations.bellows: Hrym steers from the east with shield held high, In giant-wrath does the serpent writhe; O'er the waves he twists, and the eagle squeals, The pale-beak tears corpses; Naglfar is loose.
translations.thorpe: Hrym steers from the east, he his shield raises before him; Jormungand writhes in giant-rage; the serpent beats the waves; the eagle screams; the pale-beak tears corpses; Naglfar is loosed.
translations.literal: Hrym drives from-east, raises shield before-him, turns Jormungandr in giant-rage; serpent beats waves, and eagle screams, tears corpses beak-pale, Naglfar is-loose.

original: Kjóll ferr austan, koma munu Muspells of lög lýðir, en Loki stýrir; fara fífls megir með freka öllum, þeim er bróðir Býleists í för er.

translations.bellows: A ship sails from the east, through the sea are coming Muspell's people, and Loki steers; The offspring of Fenrir all go with the wolf, And with them the brother of Byleist goes.
translations.thorpe: A ship comes from the east: Muspell's people over the sea are coming, and Loki steers; the sons of the fool [giant] are all with the wolf; Byleist's brother is with them in their course.
translations.literal: Ship goes from-east, come will Muspell's over sea people, and Loki steers; go fool's sons with wolf all, those whom brother of-Byleist [Loki] in company is.

original: Surtr ferr sunnan með sviga lævi, skín af sverði sól valtíva; grjótbjörg gnata, en gýgjar rata, troða halir helveg, en himinn klofnar.

translations.bellows: Surt fares from the south with the scourge of branches, The sun of the battle-gods shone from his sword; The crags are sundered, the giant-women sink, The dead throng Hel-way, and heaven is cloven.
translations.thorpe: Surt from the south comes with flickering flame; from his sword shines the sun of the gods of slaughtered falls. The rocky hills dash together, the giantesses totter; men tread the path of Hel, and heaven is cloven.
translations.literal: Surt goes from-south with-twigs' destruction [fire], shines from-sword sun of-slaughter-gods; rock-cliffs crash, and giantesses fall, tread men Hel-way, and heaven is-cloven.

original: Þá kemr Hlínar harmr annarr fram, er Óðinn ferr við ulf vega, en bani Belja bjartr við Surti; þá mun Friggjar falla angantýr.

translations.bellows: Then comes to Hlin yet another hurt, When Odin fares to fight with the wolf, And Beli's slayer, bright, against Surt; Then shall fall the beloved of Frigg.
translations.thorpe: Then arises Hlin's second grief, when Odin goes with the wolf to fight, and the bright slayer of Beli with Surt; then will Frigg's beloved fall.
translations.literal: Then comes Hlin's sorrow second forward, when Odin goes against wolf to-fight, and slayer of-Beli [Freyr] bright against Surt; then will Frigg's delight [Odin] fall.

original: Þá kemr inn mikli mögr Sigföður, Víðarr, vega at valdýrinu; lötr hann mögum Hveðrungs mund um standa hjör til hjarta, þá er hefnt föður.

translations.bellows: Then comes Sigfather's mighty son, Vithar, to fight with the foaming wolf; With his hand he thrusts his sword to the heart Of the giant's child, and his father is avenged.
translations.thorpe: Then comes the great son of Sieg-father, Vidar, to fight with the deadly beast. He with his hand impels his sword to the heart of the giant's son: then is his father avenged.
translations.literal: Then comes the great son of-Victory-father, Vidar, to-fight against slaughter-beast; lets he to-son of-Hvedrung [Fenrir] hand stand, sword to heart, then is avenged father.

original: Þá kemr inn mæri mögr Hlóðynjar, gengr Óðins sonr við orm vega, drepr hann af móði Miðgarðs véurr;

translations.bellows: Hither comes the son of Hlothyn, The bright one, Odin's son, to fight the serpent; In wrath he slays the warder of earth,
translations.thorpe: Then comes the great son of Hloddyn: Odin's son goes with the monster to fight; Midgard's Veor in his rage will slay it.
translations.literal: Then comes the glorious son of-Hlothyn, goes Odin's son against serpent to-fight, slays he in rage Midgard's-warder;

original: munu halir allir heimstöð ryðja, gengr fet níu Fjörgynjar burr neppr frá naðri níðs ókvíðinn.

translations.bellows: Nine paces he goes, the son of Fjorgyn, And, dying, falls from the venomous worm; will men all home-stead leave.
translations.thorpe: Nine feet will Fjorgyn's son go, bowed from the serpent, who of infamy recks not; will men all home-stead leave.
translations.literal: will men all home-stead leave, goes paces nine Fjorgyn's son, dying-fast from serpent of-shame unfearing.

original: Sól tötr svartna, sökkr fold í mar, hverfa af himni heiðar stjörnur; geisar eimr við aldurnara, leikr hár hiti við himin sjálfan.

translations.bellows: The sun turns black, earth sinks in the sea, The hot stars down from heaven are whirled; Fierce grows the steam and the life-feeding flame, Till fire leaps high about heaven itself.
translations.thorpe: The sun darkens, earth in ocean sinks, from heaven fall the bright stars, and the vapor glows with the all-nourishing fire, the heat rises high even to heaven itself.
translations.literal: Sun begins to-blacken, sinks earth in sea, vanish from heaven bright stars; rages steam with life-nourisher [fire], plays high heat against heaven itself.

original: Geyr nú Garmr mjög fyr Gnipahelli, festr mun slitna, en freki renna; fjöld veit hon fræði, fram sé ek lengra um ragnarök römm sigtíva.

translations.bellows: Loud bays Garm before Gnipahellir; The bursting fetters shall free the wolf; Much do I know, and more can see Of the fate of the gods, the mighty in fight.
translations.thorpe: Garm bays terribly before the Gnipa-cave: the fetters will be burst, and the wolf run loose. Much she knows, more can see, of the Ragnarok, of the mighty gods of victory.
translations.literal: Bays now Garm much before Cliff-cave, bond will break, and wolf run; much knows she wisdom, forward see I further of Ragnarok mighty of-victory-gods.

original: Sér hon upp koma öðru sinni jörð ór ægi iðjagræna; falla forsar, flögrar örn yfir, sá er á fjalli fiska veiðir.

translations.bellows: Now do I see the earth anew Rise all green from the waves again; The cataracts fall, and the eagle flies, And fish he catches beneath the cliffs.
translations.thorpe: She sees arise a second time earth from ocean, beauteously green, waterfalls descending; the eagle flying over, which in the mountains fishes catches.
translations.literal: Sees she up come another time earth from sea ever-green; fall waterfalls, flies eagle over, he who on mountain fish catches.

original: Hittask æsir á Iðavelli ok um moldþinur mærar dæma, ok minnask þar á megindóma ok á förnar rúnar Fimbultýs.

translations.bellows: In Ithavoll meet the Aesir again, And utter their words of the mighty world-worm; The ancient runes of Fimbultyr there They remember, and all the deeds of old.
translations.thorpe: The Aesir meet on Ida's plain, and of the mighty earth-encircler speak, and there remember their mighty deeds, and the ancient runes of Fimbultyr.
translations.literal: Meet Aesir on Field-of-Activity and about world-serpent glorious judge, and remember there of mighty-events and of ancient runes of-Mighty-God [Odin].

original: Þar munu eftir undrsamligar gullnar töflur í grasi finnask, þær er í árdaga átt höfðu.

translations.bellows: Then in the grass are golden pieces found, The wonderful games of the ancient gods, Which in the days of old they had.
translations.thorpe: There shall be again found in the grass the wonderful golden tablets, which in days of old they had possessed.
translations.literal: There will after wondrous golden tables in grass be-found, those which in early-days they had.

original: Munu ósánir akrar vaxa, böls mun batna, Baldr mun koma; búa þeir Höðr ok Baldr Hrofts sigtóftir vé valtíva. Vituð ér enn — eða hvat?

translations.bellows: Unsown shall the fields bring forth their grain, All ills grow better, and Baldr comes back; Baldr and Hoth dwell in Hropt's battle-hall, And the mighty gods: would you know yet more?
translations.thorpe: Unsown shall the fields bring forth; all evil be amended; Baldr shall come; Hodr and Baldr shall dwell in Hropt's palace of victory, the mansion of the gods of slaughter. Understand ye yet, or what?
translations.literal: Will unsown fields grow, ill will mend, Baldr will come; dwell they Hodr and Baldr in Hropt's [Odin's] victory-sites, sanctuary of-slaughter-gods. Know you yet — or what?

original: Þá kná Hænir hlautvið kjósa ok burir byggja bræðra tveggja vítt vindheim. Vituð ér enn — eða hvat?

translations.bellows: Then Hönir wins the prophetic wand, And the sons of the brothers of Tveggi abide In the windy home: would you know yet more?
translations.thorpe: Then can Hoenir choose his lot, and the sons of the two brothers dwell in the wide wind-home. Understand ye yet, or what?
translations.literal: Then can Hoenir lot-wood choose and sons build of-brothers two [Odin/Vili/Ve] wide wind-home. Know you yet — or what?

original: Sal sé hon standa sólu fegra, gulli þakðan á Gimli; þar skulu dyggvar dróttir byggja ok um aldardaga ynðis njóta.

translations.bellows: More fair than the sun, a hall I see, Roofed with gold, on Gimle it stands; There shall the righteous people dwell, And happiness ever there shall they have.
translations.thorpe: A hall she sees standing than the sun brighter, with gold bedecked, in Gimli: there shall be righteous people dwell, and for evermore happiness enjoy.
translations.literal: Hall sees she standing sun fairer, gold thatched on Gimli; there shall worthy hosts dwell and through age-days joy enjoy.

original: Þá kemr inn ríki at regindómi öflugr ofan, sá er öllu ræðr.

translations.bellows: There comes on high, all kingdoms to rule, The mighty lord, the all-powerful one.
translations.thorpe: Then comes the mighty one to the great judgment, the powerful from above, who rules over all.
translations.literal: Then comes the mighty at the-great-judgment powerful from-above, he who all rules.

original: Þar kemr inn dimmi dreki fljúgandi, naðr fránn neðan frá Niðafjöllum; berr sér í fjöðrum, flþgr völl yfir, Níðhöggr nái. Nú mun hon sökkvask.

translations.bellows: From below the dragon dark comes flying, Nithhogg from Nithafjoll over the plains; From his wings he shakes the corpses as he flies: Now she must sink.
translations.thorpe: There comes the dark dragon flying, the glistening serpent, from the Nidafells. He bears in his pinions corpses—over the plain flies Nidhogg. Now she will sink.
translations.literal: There comes the dark dragon flying, serpent glistening from-below from Dark-mountains; bears in feathers, flies field over, Nidhogg corpses. Now will she sink.
